attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old man presents to his General Practitioner with a 12 day history of pain in his left knee joint. He is having difficulty walking. He has never had something like this before. He was recently commenced on furosemide to manage heart failure. He also has a history of hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and myocardial infarction. His last HbA1c was 8.5%.

On examination, his temperature is 38.1C, blood pressure 133/90mmHg, and pulse rate 90/min. The left knee is swollen and red, with reduced range of movement. The limb is neurovascularly intact, and there are no visible ulcers.

An arthrocentesis and synovial fluid analysis are performed, which show rhomboid-shaped crystals which are positively birefringent. Synovial fluid analysis shows the following results:

  • WCC: 41,000 cells/mm³ (<200)

  • Polymorphonuclear cells: 51% (<25%)

  • Gram stain result is negative

Blood tests show that calcium, magnesium, uric acid, and phosphate levels are in the normal range.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
It is important to first recognise that this patient has calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease (CPPD), also commonly referred to as pseudogout. This is an acute condition characterised by the presence of crystals within the articular cartilage. The condition presents acutely, and tends to be idiopathic. However, notable risk factors include being above 60 years of age, and having recently begun the usage of loop diuretics. Other factors, though not present here, include damage to the joint, having had recent illness, CCAL1/CCAL2 mutations, hyperparathyroidism, hypomagnesium, and haemochromatosis.

This condition presents acutely, with symptoms/signs of pain, swelling, fever, redness, and loss of functionality/ROM. This condition is an inflammatory arthritis which can cause damage to the joint with cartilage destruction, and this occurs due to calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate deposition in the articular cartilage. This condition is diagnosed based on the presence of rhomboid-shaped crystals which are blue (parallel to the polariser) and positively birefringent under polarised light. WCC and neutrophils can expect to be elevated, and urate levels are either normal or low. On X-ray, there can be signs of chondrocalcinosis, with calcification of joint cartilage.

While gout can present relatively similarly to CPPD, there are a number of notable differences between these two conditions. Gout most commonly impacts the 1st MTP joint of the foot (though it commonly impacts the knee), crystals are negatively birefringent and yellow under polarised light (parallel to the polariser) in the shape of needles, and X-ray usually shows soft tissue swelling in acute settings and occasionally will show punched out lesions due to the presence of tophi. Gout tends to show elevated uric acid levels, although this can be normal during an acute attack. Furthermore, acute flare ups of gout last 1-2 days, whereas CPPD acute flare-ups can last for multiple days, or weeks.

While psoriatic arthritis is an inflammatory arthritis that can manifest as oligoarthritis, crystals would not be expected to be found in the synovial fluid for this form of arthritis. Furthermore, we would expect to see other features such as psoriasis, dactylitis, and we would expect to more commonly see inflammation in the spine, hands or feet.

While the patient is febrile, has a red and swollen joint, has limited ROM, has an elevated WCC and neutrophil level, and has an acute presentation, this is less likely to be septic arthritis. We would not see crystals in septic arthritis, and the Gram stain is unlikely to be negative.
learningPoints: Calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease (CPPD) can present acutely, with symptoms/signs of pain, swelling, fever, redness, and loss of functionality/ROM.

Calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease (CPPD) (also known as pseudogout) is diagnosed based on the presence of the presence of rhomboid-shaped crystals which are blue (parallel to polariser) and positively birefringent under polarised light.
references: Ryu K, Iriuchishima T, Oshida M, Kato Y, Saito A, Imada M, et al. The prevalence of and factors related to calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate crystal deposition in the knee joint. Osteoarthritis Cartilage. 2014 Jul;22(7):975–979. doi:10.1016/j.joca.2014.04.022. Available at: https://www.oarsijournal.com/article/S1063-4584(14)01060-7/fulltext

Rho YH, Zhu Y, Zhang Y, Reginato AM, Choi HK. Risk factors for pseudogout in the general population. Rheumatology (Oxford). 2012 Nov;51(11):2070–2074. doi:10.1093/rheumatology/kes204. Available at: https://academic.oup.com/rheumatology/article/51/11/2070/1777993

Cowley S, McCarthy G. Diagnosis and treatment of calcium pyrophosphate deposition (CPPD) disease: a review. Open Access Rheumatol. 2023;15:33–41. doi:10.2147/OARRR.S389664. Available at: https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/pdf/10.2147/OARRR.S389664
Clebak KT, Morrison A, Croad JR. Gout: rapid evidence review. Am Fam Physician. 2020 Nov 1;102(9):533–538. PMID: 33118789. Available at: https://www.aafp.org/pubs/afp/issues/2020/1101/p533.html

Elsissy JG, Liu JN, Wilton PJ, Nwachuku I, Gowd AK, Amin NH. Bacterial septic arthritis of the adult native knee joint: a review. JBJS Rev. 2020 Jan;8(1):e0059. doi:10.2106/JBJS.RVW.19.00059. Available at: https://europepmc.org/article/MED/31899698
